1.synthesis: The news of the Jordan crossing produces exactly what YHWH promised: the hearts of the Amorite and Canaanite kings melt and there is no longer any spirit in them. The divine warfare strategy operates at the psychological level before Israel draws a sword. This verse is the fulfillment of the eimah (supernatural dread) YHWH promised to send before Israel in Exodus 23:27 and Deuteronomy 2:25. The Jordan miracle has accomplished more than a river crossing — it has destabilized the entire military and political order of Canaan before the conquest campaign begins. The Canaanites' paralysis is YHWH's gift, creating the window for the covenant-renewal acts of circumcision and Passover before Israel engages in battle.
1.historical_context: The phrase "their hearts melted" (vayyimmas levavam) is an ANE idiom for complete psychological collapse, attested in the Amarna Letters where Canaanite vassals write to Pharaoh describing the terror YHWH's advancing people create. The Amorites west of the Jordan and the Canaanites on the coast represent the full political spectrum of Canaan's city-state system. The fact that all kings hear the news simultaneously suggests the rapid information-transmission network of the Bronze Age Levant — couriers and merchants carried political intelligence across city-states within days. The verse functions as a military intelligence report confirming that the psychological dimension of the holy-war program is already accomplished.
1.literary_structure: This single verse functions as a hinge between the Jordan crossing account and the covenant-renewal narrative of 5:2–12. It closes the crossing narrative by confirming its effect on the enemy and opens the covenant-renewal section by establishing that Israel has a window of opportunity: the enemies are paralyzed and Israel can attend to internal covenant business before battle. The identical phrase appears in Joshua 2:11 (Rahab's report) — the chapter 2 prediction is here fulfilled in chapter 5, creating a narrative echo that validates Rahab's testimony as accurate intelligence.
1.theological_application: YHWH creates the conditions for covenant renewal before battle, not after. Israel's first act in the land is not a military campaign but circumcision, Passover, and an encounter with the Commander of YHWH's army — the priorities of the covenant community in a new environment are spiritual formation before strategic engagement. The pattern recurs throughout Scripture: before Elijah confronts the prophets of Baal, he is fed and rested at Horeb (1 Kings 19); before Paul's missionary journeys, there is a period of commissioning and sending from the covenant community (Acts 13:1–3). Fear of the nations' power is answered not by tactical planning but by covenantal identity renewal.
1.christology: The melting of the Canaanite kings' hearts at YHWH's advance is a figure of what the proclamation of Christ's resurrection accomplishes: "when the report spread, this report is still told among the Jews to this day" (Matthew 28:15) — even those who seek to suppress the news of the resurrection cannot prevent its psychological impact. The "no longer any spirit in them" language prefigures Revelation 6:15–17, where the kings of the earth hide in caves when they see the Lamb coming. The same divine warrior whose name-advance paralyzed Canaan is the one whose resurrection strikes terror into every kingdom that opposes his claim. The gospel is announced in enemy territory, and its advance produces what Joshua 5:1 describes: the enemy's power dissolves before the covenant community even arrives.

2.synthesis: The mass circumcision at Gibeath-haaraloth (Hill of Foreskins) is one of the most strategically vulnerable moments in the conquest narrative: the entire fighting force is incapacitated simultaneously in enemy territory. YHWH commands this act precisely at the point when, humanly speaking, it makes the least military sense. The theological logic is absolute: Israel cannot wage holy war in an uncircumcised state. The wilderness generation — the entire adult male fighting force — was born in the wilderness and never circumcised (v5). The generation that will conquer Canaan has been uncircumcised for forty years; before the campaign begins, that covenant deficit must be addressed. The "rolling away" of the reproach of Egypt (v9) at Gilgal — galal (H1556), to roll, giving Gilgal its name — announces that Israel's slave identity is definitively removed before the land is taken.
2.historical_context: Flint knives (tzur, H6864) for circumcision reflect the deliberate use of archaic materials for sacred rites — Egyptian evidence from Saqqara shows similar use of flint for sacred procedures when iron was already available, preserving the ancient form of the covenant sign. Gibeath-haaraloth ("hill of foreskins") was likely a designated location near Gilgal for this mass procedure. The healing period would require a minimum of three to five days before the men could walk normally and longer before they could fight — making Israel entirely defenseless during this window. The "reproach of Egypt" encompasses both the shame of slavery and the uncircumcised status that marked the wilderness generation as outside full covenant standing. The Passover sequence in vv10–12 follows immediately — circumcision restored covenant eligibility for Passover participation (Exodus 12:48).
2.literary_structure: The circumcision narrative (vv2–9) is framed by two divine commands (vv2, 9) with the procedure and its rationale between them (vv3–8). The Gilgal etymology (v9) provides the chapter's interpretive key: "this day I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you." The place-name perpetuates the covenant act in Israel's geography. The sequence — circumcision (vv2–9), Passover (vv10–12), manna cessation (v12), Commander encounter (vv13–15) — creates a covenant-renewal cluster at Gilgal before any battle is fought.
2.theological_application: YHWH's command to circumcise in enemy territory forces Israel to trust divine protection during their greatest vulnerability. This is the fundamental shape of covenant obedience: doing what the covenant requires even when doing it is strategically dangerous. The "reproach of Egypt" that is rolled away is not merely a memory of past slavery — it is the spiritual status of living outside full covenant identity. Every generation must attend to its own covenant standing before engaging in the work of the kingdom. Paul's language in Galatians 3:23 about the law as a guardian during a period of immaturity resonates: the wilderness was Israel's period of guarded immaturity; the crossing into the land begins the mature covenant life that requires the full covenant sign to be in place.
2.christology: Paul in Colossians 2:11–12 describes baptism as "the circumcision of Christ — a circumcision made without hands" — the new covenant version of the Gilgal act. The cutting away of the flesh (Colossians 2:11) accomplished in Christ's death removes not the foreskin but "the body of the flesh" — the entire old-humanity status. The Gilgal mass circumcision thus prefigures the new covenant's initiating act: entry into the covenant community through a death-and-burial sign (Colossians 2:12). The "rolling away" (galal) of reproach at Gilgal anticipates Galatians 3:13 — "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law" — the same rolling-away atonement accomplished not with flint knives but through the cross. The healing period of three to five days embedded in the account has attracted typological reading as the three-day burial of Christ, who was raised on the third day to begin the new covenant campaign.

10.synthesis: The Passover at Gilgal is the first Passover celebrated in the land of Canaan — and the last Passover on manna. The day after the Passover feast, Israel eats of the produce of the land, and the manna ceases. The transition is momentous: the supernatural wilderness provision gives way to the natural abundance of the promised land, itself YHWH's gift but now mediated through the land's own fertility. The cessation of manna is not withdrawal of provision but its transformation: YHWH provides in the land through the land, not through daily bread from heaven. The Passover in the land completes the Exodus journey: Israel was redeemed from Egypt at the first Passover; they eat the first Passover in their inheritance at the land's threshold.
10.historical_context: The fourteenth of Nisan Passover (v10) corresponds to the Exodus 12 calendar. The produce of the land — grain parched or as unleavened cakes (v11) — is the earliest-available food from the Canaanite fields, eaten at the Passover and the days immediately following. Manna began in the second month after the Exodus (Exodus 16:1) and continued through forty years of wilderness wandering. Its cessation "on the day after the Passover" (v12) is chronologically precise: forty years and one month of supernatural provision ends at the moment the natural provision of the land becomes available. This is not a coincidence but a divine handoff, marking the transition between two eras of covenant provision.
10.literary_structure: The Passover, manna-cessation, and produce-of-the-land sequence (vv10–12) creates a three-panel transition tableau: the redemption memorial (Passover), the end of wilderness provision (manna), and the beginning of land provision (Canaanite grain). The compression of these three events into three verses signals their theological unity — they are one moment of covenant transformation, not three separate events. The phrase "the day after the Passover" appears once for the manna cessation and once for the eating of the land's produce, making them simultaneous, both on Nisan 15.
10.theological_application: The manna's cessation teaches that YHWH's modes of provision change with the season of redemptive history, but his faithfulness does not. The wilderness generation needed daily visible supernatural provision because they were in a place where no natural provision was available and their faith was being formed. The land generation will need a different kind of trust: cultivating the land, waiting for rain, operating within the natural order that YHWH governs providentially. The same God who sent manna sends rain. The temptation in the land will be to attribute the grain to Baal's fertility rather than YHWH's providential care — the Passover immediately before the manna's end is the theological anchor that holds the land's provision within the Exodus framework.
10.christology: "Christ our Passover is sacrificed" (1 Corinthians 5:7) — the first Passover in the land is the shadow of the definitive Passover accomplished at the cross. The manna that ceases points forward to what replaces it: Jesus's teaching in John 6:49–58 — "your fathers ate manna in the wilderness and they died... I am the bread of life; whoever eats of this bread will live forever" — places Christ as the true manna who does not cease. The Gilgal Passover's transition from supernatural manna to natural land-produce is itself a figure of the transition from the old covenant's provisional provisions to the new covenant's permanent feast: "unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you" (John 6:53). The Eucharist is the new covenant Gilgal Passover — celebrating redemption while eating the produce of the age to come.

13.pericope_label: The Commander of the Lord's Army
13.synthesis: Joshua's encounter with the Commander of YHWH's army near Jericho is the pivot on which the entire conquest turns. Joshua's question — "Are you for us or for our adversaries?" — receives an answer that refuses his binary: "No; I am the Commander of the army of YHWH; I have now come." The divine warrior has not arrived on Israel's side; rather, Israel finds itself on the side of the divine warrior who commands the heavenly armies independently of human alliances. The command to remove sandals (v15) signals that the ground Joshua stands on is holy — the identical command given to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:5) — identifying this Commander as the same divine presence who commissioned Moses. The conquest of Canaan begins not with tactical planning but with Joshua prostrate before the Commander of heaven's armies on holy ground.
13.historical_context: The sar-tzva-YHWH (Commander of YHWH's army, H8269 + H6635) appears only here as a title in the OT. The figure holds a drawn sword — the battle posture, not the peace posture. The command to remove sandals is an exact verbal parallel to Exodus 3:5: "remove your sandals from your feet, for the place where you stand is holy ground." This precise quotation identifies the Commander with the theophanic figure who appeared to Moses at Horeb, establishing continuity between the Exodus commissioning and the conquest commissioning. The Gilgal base camp near Jericho is the site of Israel's most concentrated covenant-renewal activity — the holy presence extends beyond Mount Sinai to wherever YHWH manifests himself.
13.literary_structure: The encounter is brief — four verses — but structurally decisive. It stands between the covenant-renewal cluster (circumcision, Passover, manna-cessation) and the Jericho battle-instructions (6:2–5), functioning as the divine commissioning that validates what follows. Joshua's initial challenge question and the Commander's refusal of the binary creates the narrative's theological hinge: the conquest is not Israel's campaign with divine assistance, but YHWH's campaign in which Israel participates under divine command. The prostration and sandal-removal (v14b–15) echo Moses's Sinai encounter and mark the transition from Joshua's identity as military leader to Joshua's identity as subordinate of the divine Commander.
13.theological_application: "Are you for us or for our adversaries?" is the question every covenant community is tempted to ask when facing opposition. The divine answer — "No; I am the Commander of YHWH's army" — redirects the question entirely: the issue is not whose side YHWH is on but whether the community is on YHWH's side. The American theologian Abraham Lincoln grasped this in his response during the Civil War: "I do not pray that God is on our side, but that we are on God's side." The removal of sandals signals that the covenant community's first task before any engagement is worship — coming before the Holy One in appropriate reverence rather than treating divine support as a given.
13.christology: The Commander of YHWH's army has been consistently identified by the Christian tradition as a Christophany — the pre-incarnate Son appearing in visible form. The grounds are strong: he receives Joshua's worship without redirecting it (unlike angels, who refuse worship: Revelation 22:8–9), he commands the removal of sandals as God commands Moses (Exodus 3:5), and in 6:2 it is "YHWH" who speaks the Jericho battle-instructions that follow immediately. Jesus's claim in John 8:58 — "before Abraham was, I am" — places the eternal Son before the Abrahamic covenant; his presence at Jericho as the divine Commander fits this pre-incarnate activity. Revelation 19:11–16 presents the returning Christ as a warrior on a white horse, the armies of heaven following him — the same Commander of YHWH's army, now revealed in his final form. The holy ground at Jericho is the first installment of a claim that culminates in the new earth, where YHWH's presence makes everything holy.
